title: Liquid Bread: The Grain That Built Civilization
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why beer matters to history and how this season studies it through four lenses.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Good morning. This is Anthony McDonald, and welcome to Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. Season 1 followed the plants of the daily ritual. This season follows fermented and distilled drinks, and we start at the beginning, with beer.

[HOOK] A quick, honest note first. This is a history and economics class, not a bar. We study beer because it is one of the oldest and most influential things humans make, and we will be straight about its harms too. Nothing here is encouragement to drink. Now the hook. The oldest written recipe on Earth is a recipe for beer, the Hymn to Ninkasi, carved on a clay tablet around 1800 BCE (Civil, 1964). And some archaeologists argue that the desire for beer may have helped pull our ancestors into farming and settling down in the first place. People may have planted grain to brew before they planted it to bake.

[TEACH] We read beer through four lenses. Geography asks where barley and hops grow, and why the rock under a town decides what beer it makes. Social studies asks how beer organized the first cities, fed medieval monasteries, and triggered the failed experiment of Prohibition. Economics asks who gets paid for a pint, and why a small local brewery does more for its town than a giant one. English language arts asks what a 4,000-year-old hymn, a Prohibition pamphlet, and a craft-beer label each reveal. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Beer is not a modern indulgence. It is older than writing, tangled up with the birth of cities, and it carries geography, history, economics, and language inside every glass. That is why we call it liquid bread.

[CHECK] Quick check. What is the Hymn to Ninkasi, and why is it remarkable? [Beat] It is the oldest known written recipe, a Sumerian prayer to the beer goddess that doubles as a brewing manual, from around 1800 BCE (Civil, 1964).

## Key Takeaways
- Season 2 of Better Vice Club covers fermented and distilled drinks, starting with beer.
- The episode is educational, honest about alcohol's harms, and not an endorsement of drinking.
- The oldest known written recipe, the Hymn to Ninkasi (about 1800 BCE), is a beer recipe (Civil, 1964).
- Better Vice Club reads beer through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts.

## Sources
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago.

title: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can describe what barley and hops need to grow and where those belts lie.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we set up the season. Two quick questions.
One: what is the oldest known written recipe? [Beat] The Hymn to Ninkasi, a beer recipe (Civil, 1964).
Two: what nickname does beer get for its calories and nutrition? [Beat] Liquid bread.

[HOOK] Beer is mostly two crops and some water. Get the two crops right and you can brew. But each one grows in a particular band of the world, and that geography quietly decides where great beer comes from.

[TEACH] Start with the grain. The main grain in beer is barley, Hordeum vulgare, and barley wants a temperate climate, growing best between about 35 and 55 degrees North (Newton et al., 2011). It needs a growing season of about 90 to 120 frost-free days, moderate rainfall, and, oddly, it does best in soil that is not too rich. Barley likes a challenge. That is why a place like Indiana, which sits inside the barley latitudes, grows mostly corn and soybeans instead, its soil is too fertile for barley's taste.
Now the flavoring. Hops, Humulus lupulus, are the flower that gives beer its bitterness, aroma, and a natural preservative power (Rybacek, 1991). Hops are even pickier than barley. They want long summer days, 14 hours or more of daylight, a real winter dormancy, and rich, well-drained soil, which puts the hop belt at about 35 to 55 degrees, north and south. That is why the famous hop regions, the Yakima Valley in Washington, the Hallertau in Germany, are all in that temperate band.
So the raw materials of beer are not global. They are temperate. Barley and hops both belong to the same middle latitudes, which is why the great brewing traditions grew up in northern Europe and the northern United States.

[PICTURE] Picture two green bands wrapped around the upper middle of the globe, one for barley, one for hops, both sitting between about 35 and 55 degrees. Almost every classic brewing country, Germany, Czechia, England, the northern United States, falls inside those bands. Beer is a child of the temperate world.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: beer's two key crops, barley and hops, both grow best in the temperate band between about 35 and 55 degrees, and barley even prefers poorer soil (Newton et al., 2011; Rybacek, 1991). That shared geography is why the great beer cultures cluster in the cooler middle latitudes.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does Indiana, which sits within the barley latitudes, grow little barley? [Beat] Its soil is too rich and fertile, and barley does better in poorer soil (Newton et al., 2011).

## Key Takeaways
- Barley (Hordeum vulgare), beer's main grain, grows best between about 35 and 55 degrees North and prefers poorer soil (Newton et al., 2011).
- Hops (Humulus lupulus) give bitterness, aroma, and preservation, and need long summer days and a winter dormancy (Rybacek, 1991).
- Both belong to the temperate band, roughly 35 to 55 degrees, north or south.
- That shared geography is why the great brewing traditions grew up in the cooler middle latitudes.

## Sources
- Newton, A. C., Flavell, A. J., George, T. S., Leat, P., Mullholland, B., Ramsay, L., & Swanston, J. S. (2011). Crops that feed the world 4. Barley: A resilient crop? Food Security, 3(2), 141-178.
- Rybacek, V. (Ed.). (1991). Hop production. Elsevier.

title: How Geology Makes the Beer
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how the minerals in local water, set by geology, shape which beer styles a place can brew.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped the grain and hop belts. Two quick questions.
One: in what latitude band do barley and hops grow best? [Beat] About 35 to 55 degrees (Newton et al., 2011).
Two: why does barley like poorer soil? [Beat] It does best with a challenge, not rich soil (Newton et al., 2011).

[HOOK] Here is the deepest idea in beer geography. The single biggest reason a city became famous for one style of beer and not another is not the climate or the grain. It is the rock under the ground, because the rock decides what is in the water.

[TEACH] Beer is mostly water, and water is never pure. As rain soaks through the ground, it dissolves minerals from the rock, and those minerals change what beer the water can make (Bamforth, 2009). Four classic brewing cities show it perfectly.
Plzen, in the Czech Republic, sits on sandstone, which gives very soft water, low in everything. Soft water makes a pale, crisp, clear beer, and that is exactly the Pilsner that Plzen gave its name to (Bamforth, 2009). Dublin, in Ireland, sits on limestone, which gives hard water rich in calcium bicarbonate. That water suits dark, roasty beer, which is why Dublin became a city of stout. Burton-upon-Trent, in England, sits on gypsum, giving very hard water full of calcium sulfate, and sulfate sharpens hop bitterness, which is why Burton became the home of the bitter, hoppy India Pale Ale. And Munich, in Germany, with its moderately soft water over glacial ground, makes a balanced, malty lager.
See the pattern? Nobody sat down and chose these styles. The brewers worked with the water they had, and the water came from the rock. The Pilsner, the stout, and the IPA are, in a real sense, made of geology.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Four cities, four kinds of rock underneath. Soft sandstone water at Plzen turns pale and crisp. Hard limestone water at Dublin turns dark and roasty. Gypsum water at Burton turns sharp and bitter. The beer in the glass is a reading of the stone below the town.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the minerals in local water, set by the underlying rock, decide which beer styles a place can brew, so Plzen made Pilsner, Dublin made stout, and Burton made IPA (Bamforth, 2009). Beer style is geology you can taste.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why did Dublin become a city of dark, roasty stout? [Beat] Its hard limestone water, rich in calcium bicarbonate, suits dark, roasty beer (Bamforth, 2009).

## Key Takeaways
- Beer is mostly water, and water dissolves minerals from the local rock, which shapes the beer (Bamforth, 2009).
- Plzen's soft sandstone water makes pale, crisp Pilsner; Dublin's hard limestone water suits dark stout (Bamforth, 2009).
- Burton's gypsum water, high in sulfate, sharpens hop bitterness, giving the IPA.
- Brewers worked with the water they had, so classic styles are, in a real sense, made of geology.

## Sources
- Bamforth, C. W. (2009). Beer: Tap into the art and science of brewing (3rd ed.). Oxford University Press.

title: Beer Geography Is Shifting
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how climate change is moving the geography of beer.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 5 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned that geology shapes beer style. Two quick questions.
One: what kind of water made Plzen's Pilsner? [Beat] Soft water from sandstone (Bamforth, 2009).
Two: what mineral in Burton's water sharpens hop bitterness? [Beat] Sulfate, from gypsum (Bamforth, 2009).

[HOOK] The grain belt and the hop belt are not fixed lines on a map. They are moving, slowly, as the climate warms, and that is starting to change where beer can be made and how good it is.

[TEACH] Researchers tracking beer and climate have found several shifts already underway (Mika et al., 2023). Barley-growing regions are creeping northward, by an estimated 4 to 8 kilometers per decade, as the warmer edge of the belt becomes too hot and the cooler edge opens up. Hop quality has declined in some traditional European regions, where rising temperatures stress the plants and lower the prized aroma compounds. Water, which beer needs in large amounts, is getting scarcer in key hop-growing areas of the American West.
And there is a flip side. As the cool edge of the belt warms, new places become possible. Parts of Scotland, Scandinavia, and Canada that were once too cold are now viable for growing hops (Mika et al., 2023). So the map is not just shrinking, it is sliding, north.
This connects back to the rest of the season. Beer depends on temperate crops and on steady water, and both are being reshaped by a warming world. The geology under a brewing town does not change, but the climate and water around it do, and that is enough to move the whole industry.

[PICTURE] Picture the two green belts from our first geography lesson slowly drifting toward the poles. The southern edge browns and dries out. The northern edge greens up, reaching into Scotland and Scandinavia. Same crops, but the band they grow in is migrating north, decade by decade.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: climate change is sliding beer's geography northward, with barley regions moving about 4 to 8 kilometers per decade, hop quality falling in parts of Europe, and new regions opening in the far north (Mika et al., 2023). The map of beer is on the move.

[CHECK] Quick check. Name one way climate change is altering beer geography. [Beat] Any of these: barley regions moving north, hop quality declining in Europe, water scarcity in the American West, or new hop regions opening in Scotland, Scandinavia, and Canada (Mika et al., 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Barley-growing regions are moving northward by an estimated 4 to 8 kilometers per decade (Mika et al., 2023).
- Hop quality has declined in some traditional European regions as temperatures rise (Mika et al., 2023).
- Water scarcity threatens key hop areas in the American West.
- New regions in Scotland, Scandinavia, and Canada are becoming viable, so the beer map is sliding north.

## Sources
- Mika, M., et al. (2023). Climate change and beer production. Nature Plants.

title: Liquid Bread and the First Cities
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain beer's role in early civilization and how it was woven into law and daily life.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned that beer geography is moving. Two quick questions.
One: name one way climate change is moving beer geography. [Beat] Any of these: barley regions creeping north, hop quality falling in parts of Europe, water scarcity in the American West, or new regions opening in Scotland and Scandinavia (Mika et al., 2023).
Two: what is the latitude band where barley grows best? [Beat] Roughly 35 to 55 degrees North (Newton et al., 2011).

[HOOK] We just spent a few lessons on where beer grows. Now flip the question. What did beer do to the people who made it? The honest answer is strange and large. Wet grain may have helped pull our ancestors out of the wandering life and into the first towns, and once they settled, beer showed up in their oldest laws.

[TEACH] Start with the deep timeline. Grain cultivation in the Fertile Crescent goes back to around 10,000 BCE. That is the usual story of how farming began. But some archaeologists argue the motive was not bread at all. They think the pull of fermented grain, beer, helped convince hunter-gatherers to stop moving and stay near the fields they planted. We cannot prove the motive. We can show the drink is old.

The earliest direct evidence sits in China, not the Middle East. At a site called Jiahu, chemical residue of a fermented grain drink dates to around 7000 BCE (Hornsey, 2003). That is beer, or a close cousin of it, older than many of the civilizations we usually name first. By around 3400 BCE beer was a settled fact of Sumerian life, showing up in their seals and their art, woven into religion and the economy.

Now the part that makes the social story click. When Mesopotamia wrote down its laws, beer was in them. The Code of Hammurabi, from around 1754 BCE, is one of the oldest comprehensive legal codes we have, and it spends real attention on beer (King, 1910). That alone tells you something. A society does not regulate what it does not care about. Beer mattered enough to put in the law.

Look at what those laws actually did. First, beer was pay. Workers received daily beer rations, and the amount rose with rank. Common laborers got roughly 2 liters a day. Skilled workers got more. Managers and officials got up to about 5 liters (King, 1910). Beer was not an occasional treat. It was wages, food, and drink at once.

Second, the law set prices. Tavern keepers who overcharged for beer faced harsh punishment. Watering down the beer was treated as a serious offense, not a small cheat (King, 1910). So the code protected the buyer, the way a modern food-safety or fair-pricing rule does. The same instinct, thousands of years earlier.

Third, the tavern itself was an institution. It was a community gathering place, a spot to trade news and do business, not just a place to drink (Hornsey, 2003). Beer organized where people met.

[PICTURE] Picture a clay tablet, hard and dry, pressed with wedge-shaped marks. On it are rules about a drink. A laborer lines up after a day of work and is handed a measured jar of beer as part of his pay. The tavern keeper next door knows exactly what she can charge, because the price is fixed by the king's law, and watering the beer down could cost her dearly. That is beer running through the bones of one of the first cities.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: beer is ancient, with fermented-grain residue at Jiahu in China around 7000 BCE, and by the time of the Code of Hammurabi around 1754 BCE it was paid as wages, price-controlled, and protected from dilution by law (King, 1910; Hornsey, 2003). Beer was infrastructure, not just a beverage.

[CHECK] Quick check. What do the beer rules in the Code of Hammurabi tell us about beer's place in Mesopotamian society? [Beat] They show beer was central enough to regulate. It was used as worker pay (about 2 to 5 liters a day by rank), its price was controlled, and watering it down was a serious offense, so beer was food, wages, and a social institution at once (King, 1910).

## Key Takeaways
- Grain cultivation in the Fertile Crescent dates to around 10,000 BCE, and some archaeologists argue beer helped motivate people to settle down.
- The earliest direct beer-residue evidence comes from Jiahu, China, around 7000 BCE (Hornsey, 2003).
- By around 3400 BCE beer was embedded in Sumerian religious and economic life.
- The Code of Hammurabi, around 1754 BCE, paid workers in beer (about 2 to 5 liters a day by rank), set tavern price controls, and punished watering it down (King, 1910).
- Taverns were community gathering places, so beer helped organize early city life.

## Sources
- King, L. W. (1910). The Code of Hammurabi. Yale Law School Avalon Project.
- Hornsey, I. S. (2003). A history of beer and brewing. Royal Society of Chemistry.

title: The Monks Who Kept Brewing
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why medieval monasteries became centers of brewing and what the Trappist model preserves.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we visited beer in the first cities. Two quick questions.
One: what do the beer rules in the Code of Hammurabi show about beer? [Beat] That it was central to society. It was paid as wages, price-controlled, and watering it down was a serious offense (King, 1910).
Two: where is the earliest direct beer-residue evidence, and roughly when? [Beat] Jiahu, in China, around 7000 BCE (Hornsey, 2003).

[HOOK] After Rome, a lot of practical knowledge in Europe could have been lost. Brewing was not, and a big reason is a quiet group of people who treated work as prayer. For more than a thousand years, monasteries kept brewing alive, and a small handful of abbeys still brew the old way today.

[TEACH] From about 500 to 1800 CE, monasteries were major centers of brewing in Europe, and there were good reasons (Unger, 2004). Start with water. City water was often unsafe to drink. Beer, because it was fermented and usually boiled in the making, was safer than the water around it. A monastery that brewed could keep its people from getting sick.

Next, nutrition. Beer earned the nickname liquid bread. It carried calories, carbohydrates, and B vitamins, which made it genuinely useful during fasting, when monks ate little solid food (Unger, 2004). A drink that fed you was a real advantage on a fast day.

Then money. Monasteries sold beer, and the income funded charity and education. Brewing paid for the work the community did in the world. And there was a spiritual frame over all of it. The Benedictine motto ora et labora, "pray and work," made productive labor itself a form of devotion (Hieronymus, 2005). Brewing was not a distraction from holy life. It was part of it.

Around the edges of this same world sits a famous law. In 1516, Bavaria enacted the Reinheitsgebot, an early food-safety rule that defined beer as water, barley, and hops (Unger, 2004). Yeast was added to the definition later, once people understood it. The point for us is that beer was being protected and standardized as a serious food, the way the Code of Hammurabi had protected it long before.

Now jump to today, because a thin thread of the monastic tradition survives. The Trappist model is strict. Only a small number of certified abbeys may call their beer Trappist, and the rules are specific (Hieronymus, 2005). The beer must be brewed within the monastery walls. Monks, or lay brothers under their watch, must be involved in making it. The revenue must support the monastic community, and surplus must fund charity. And they do not advertise. The production is sized to the community's needs, no more.

To feel how different that is from the rest of the beer world, hold two breweries side by side. Westvleteren XII is a tiny Trappist beer, rated by many as one of the best in the world, made in small batches you can barely get. Now picture a giant like AB InBev. The gap in how much they make runs on the order of 1 to 125,000 (Hieronymus, 2005). Same product, beer, at completely different scales and for completely different reasons.

[PICTURE] Picture a stone brewery inside monastery walls. Bells, quiet, a few brothers tending a kettle. The beer they make is partly food for fasting days, partly safe drink, partly income for the poor at the gate. No billboard, no ad. Now picture a global brewery with trucks running day and night to fifty countries. Set the tiny abbey beside the giant and the ratio is roughly 1 to 125,000. Both are beer. Only one is also prayer.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: monasteries brewed from about 500 to 1800 CE because beer was safer than the water, fed people as liquid bread, funded charity, and fit the motto ora et labora, and the Trappist model still requires brewing within the walls, monk involvement, community funding, and no advertising (Unger, 2004; Hieronymus, 2005).

[CHECK] Quick check. Name two things the Trappist model requires today. [Beat] Any two of these: the beer must be brewed within the monastery walls, monks or lay brothers must be involved, the revenue must support the community and fund charity, and there is no advertising (Hieronymus, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- Monasteries were brewing centers from about 500 to 1800 CE because beer was safer than unsafe city water (Unger, 2004).
- Beer as liquid bread gave calories and B vitamins, useful during fasting, and brewing income funded charity and education (Unger, 2004).
- The Benedictine motto ora et labora made brewing a spiritual act (Hieronymus, 2005).
- The 1516 Reinheitsgebot in Bavaria defined beer as water, barley, and hops, an early food-safety law (Unger, 2004).
- The Trappist model requires brewing within the walls, monk involvement, community funding, and no advertising, and tiny Westvleteren XII against a giant like AB InBev runs on the order of 1 to 125,000 (Hieronymus, 2005).

## Sources
- Unger, R. W. (2004). Beer in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance. University of Pennsylvania Press.
- Hieronymus, S. (2005). Brew like a monk: Trappist, abbey, and strong Belgian ales and how to brew them. Brewers Publications.

title: Prohibition, the Experiment That Failed
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze why the United States tried Prohibition and what its results teach.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we followed brewing into the monasteries. Two quick questions.
One: why did medieval monasteries brew beer? [Beat] Any of these: city water was unsafe and beer was safer, beer was liquid bread that fed people during fasting, brewing income funded charity, and work counted as prayer under ora et labora (Unger, 2004; Hieronymus, 2005).
Two: name one thing the Trappist model requires today. [Beat] Any of these: brewed within the monastery walls, monks involved, revenue supports the community and charity, and no advertising (Hieronymus, 2005).

[HOOK] Most of this episode celebrates beer. This lesson is the hard part. The United States once tried to ban alcohol outright, for thirteen years, and it is worth taking seriously, because the people who pushed for it were responding to real harm, and the policy still went wrong.

[TEACH] Start with the problem, honestly. The temperance movement was not built on nothing. Alcohol abuse was tied to domestic violence, to poverty, and to workplace accidents (Okrent, 2010). The movement was often led by women, and that matters. In an era when women had few legal protections, a husband's drinking could wreck a family, and the law gave a wife almost nowhere to turn. Wanting that fixed was reasonable. The grievance was real.

Now the legal mechanics. The Eighteenth Amendment took effect in 1920 and banned the manufacture, sale, and transport of intoxicating liquor (Okrent, 2010). The Volstead Act did the enforcing and set the line, defining "intoxicating" at just 0.5 percent alcohol, low enough to catch nearly everything. Thirteen years later the country reversed itself. The Twenty-First Amendment repealed Prohibition in 1933, and here is a detail worth remembering: it is the only Constitutional amendment ever repealed (Okrent, 2010). The nation wrote a ban into its highest law, then took it back out.

Now the results, by the numbers. Licensed breweries in the United States fell from about 1,300 before Prohibition to near zero under it (Okrent, 2010). Federal tax revenue from alcohol went to zero, money the government had counted on, simply gone. And the demand did not vanish. It went underground, and organized crime grew explosively to supply it. After repeal, that criminal activity declined, because the legal market took its business back (Okrent, 2010).

So weigh it. Did Prohibition cut drinking? Somewhat, for a while. Did it solve the harm the temperance movement cared about? No. It created new harms instead. The lawful, taxed, visible beer trade was replaced by an unlawful, untaxed, violent one. That is the honest policy lesson here. A ban does not erase demand. It moves demand somewhere you can no longer see or control, and the people running that hidden market answer to no one.

[PICTURE] Picture two charts side by side. On the left, the number of legal breweries: about 1,300, then a cliff straight down to near zero in 1920. On the right, organized crime: a low line that suddenly shoots upward through the 1920s, then bends back down after 1933. The two charts are the same story told twice. Push the legal trade down and the illegal trade rises to take its place.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Prohibition answered real harm but failed as policy. The Eighteenth Amendment (1920) and the Volstead Act, with its 0.5 percent line, banned alcohol, and the Twenty-First Amendment repealed it in 1933, the only amendment ever repealed; meanwhile breweries fell from about 1,300 to near zero, alcohol tax revenue went to zero, and organized crime grew explosively (Okrent, 2010).

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is Prohibition usually judged a failed experiment, even though the movement behind it responded to real harm? [Beat] Because it did not remove demand. It drove the trade underground, sent breweries from about 1,300 to near zero, cut alcohol tax revenue to zero, and fueled explosive growth in organized crime, so it created new harms rather than fixing the old ones (Okrent, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The temperance movement, often led by women, responded to real harm: alcohol abuse linked to domestic violence, poverty, and workplace accidents (Okrent, 2010).
- The Eighteenth Amendment (1920) and the Volstead Act, which set a 0.5 percent threshold, banned alcohol nationwide (Okrent, 2010).
- The Twenty-First Amendment repealed Prohibition in 1933, the only Constitutional amendment ever repealed (Okrent, 2010).
- Licensed breweries fell from about 1,300 to near zero and federal alcohol tax revenue went to zero (Okrent, 2010).
- Organized crime grew explosively under Prohibition, then declined after repeal, so the ban moved harm rather than ending it (Okrent, 2010).

## Sources
- Okrent, D. (2010). Last call: The rise and fall of Prohibition. Scribner.

title: Who Gets Paid for a Pint
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can trace where the money goes across the beer supply chain and explain why the three-tier system exists.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at Prohibition, the experiment that failed. Two quick questions.
One: which amendment to the Constitution is the only one ever repealed? [Beat] The Eighteenth Amendment, which started Prohibition, repealed by the Twenty-First in 1933.
Two: name one result of Prohibition by the numbers. [Beat] The number of legal breweries fell from about 1,300 before to near zero during, and organized crime grew fast.

[HOOK] Pick up a pint of beer and ask a simple question. You paid for this. So who got the money? It feels like one purchase, but that single pint passed through a long line of hands, a farmer, a maltster, a brewer, a driver, a bartender, and the government. Each one took a slice. Let me walk you down the line.

[TEACH] Start in the field. The barley farmer who grew the grain gets only about 3 to 5 percent of what you pay for the pint (Brewers Association, 2024). The hop grower, who raised the flower that gives the beer its bitterness, gets even less, about 1 to 2 percent. Then the malting company, which sprouts and roasts the grain so it is ready to brew, takes about 3 to 4 percent.
Now the brewing itself. The brewery, where grain and water and yeast and hops become beer, captures a big share, about 25 to 35 percent of the pint price. After brewing comes the trucks. Distribution, the wholesalers who move the beer from the brewery to the bar, takes about 20 to 25 percent. And the final stop, the retail or the taproom, the bar or store that hands you the glass, takes the largest slice of all, about 30 to 40 percent. Last in line is the government. Tax, federal and state, accounts for about 10 to 15 percent of the pint (Brewers Association, 2024).
Here is the part worth holding onto. A craft brewery with its own taproom is doing two jobs at once. It is the brewer and it is the retailer, so it captures both the brewing slice and the retail slice in the same pint. That is why selling a beer across the bar at the taproom is so much more profitable for a small brewery than shipping it out through distributors. The brewery keeps the share that would otherwise go to a store.
Now the tax, because there is a clever wrinkle. The federal government charges an excise tax, a tax on the beer itself, by the barrel. A barrel is 31 gallons. A small brewer pays only about 3 dollars and 50 cents per barrel on the first 60,000 barrels (Brewers Association, 2024). A large producer pays about 18 dollars per barrel. So the tax code gives the little brewer a real break on the first chunk of beer, to help small breweries get started.
And one more piece, the rule that shapes the whole chain. In most states, after Prohibition ended, the law set up the three-tier system. It says beer must pass through three separate companies. The brewer sells to a distributor, the distributor sells to a retailer, and only then does the retailer sell to you. The brewer cannot just own the bar and sell straight to drinkers. Why force three tiers? Because before Prohibition, breweries owned the saloons outright, the old tied-house setup, and they pushed people to drink hard to move their own beer. Splitting the chain into three was meant to break that monopoly and stop the abuse. It works, but it adds a cost, and it falls hardest on small brewers who would rather deliver their own beer.

[PICTURE] Picture your pint passing down a line of seven hands. The farmer and the hop grower at the front each pinch off a few pennies. The maltster takes a small handful. The brewer grabs a big handful, the trucks grab another, and the bar grabs the biggest of all. The government takes the last pinch. Now picture a craft taproom, where the brewer and the bar are the same person, so two of those handfuls land in one pocket.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: across a pint, farmers get only about 3 to 5 percent, the brewery about 25 to 35 percent, and the retailer the most at about 30 to 40 percent, while the three-tier system forces beer through brewer, distributor, and retailer to stop the old tied-house monopolies (Brewers Association, 2024). The money is spread thin at the farm and stacked high at the bar.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does a craft brewery make more on a beer sold in its own taproom than on one shipped through distributors? [Beat] Because in its taproom the brewery is both the brewer and the retailer, so it captures both the brewing slice and the retail slice of the pint (Brewers Association, 2024).

## Key Takeaways
- Across a pint, barley farmers get about 3 to 5 percent and hop growers about 1 to 2 percent (Brewers Association, 2024).
- The brewery captures about 25 to 35 percent, distribution about 20 to 25 percent, and retail or taproom the most at about 30 to 40 percent, with tax about 10 to 15 percent (Brewers Association, 2024).
- A craft taproom captures both the brewing and retail margins, which is why taproom sales are more profitable than distribution.
- Federal excise tax runs about 3 dollars and 50 cents per barrel on a small brewer's first 60,000 barrels and about 18 dollars per barrel for large producers, with a barrel being 31 gallons (Brewers Association, 2024).
- Most states require the three-tier system, brewer to distributor to retailer, to prevent the old tied-house monopolies, though it adds cost for small brewers.

## Sources
- Brewers Association. (2024). National beer sales and production data. https://www.brewersassociation.org/statistics-and-data/

title: Macro Beer and Craft Beer
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can contrast giant and craft brewing and explain why local breweries do more for their towns.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we traced who gets paid for a pint. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what share of a pint does the barley farmer get? [Beat] Only about 3 to 5 percent (Brewers Association, 2024).
Two: why does the three-tier system exist? [Beat] To force beer through brewer, distributor, and retailer and prevent the old tied-house monopolies that breweries used to run.

[HOOK] There are two very different kinds of beer company, and they live in almost opposite worlds. One is a giant, brewing oceans of beer and spending fortunes to put its name in front of you. The other is small, brewing a little, and trusting that you will tell a friend. They make the same drink, but they run on completely different math, and that math decides what your town gets out of every dollar you spend.

[TEACH] Start with the giants, macro brewing. A handful of huge companies, the biggest being AB InBev, hold roughly 70 percent of all the beer volume in the United States (Brewers Association, 2024). These companies spend billions of dollars a year on advertising, the kind you see during the big game. Their whole model runs on volume. They sell so much beer that even a thin profit on each can adds up to enormous money. To make that work, they chase scale, buy up rivals, and push their cans into every store and stadium in the country.
Now the other world, craft brewing. There are more than 9,500 small craft breweries in the United States, and together they hold about 25 percent of the beer volume (Brewers Association, 2024). A small brewery cannot outspend a giant on advertising, so it does not try. It leans on its taproom, on selling beer across its own bar, and on word of mouth, one drinker telling the next. Its model is the opposite of the giant's. Not oceans of beer at a thin margin, but a smaller amount sold closer to home, where the brewery keeps more of the price.
Here is why that difference matters to your town. Economists talk about the local multiplier effect, which is just how many times a dollar bounces around your community before it leaves. Spend money at a local craft brewery and that dollar comes back around roughly 2 to 4 times in local economic activity (Brewers Association, 2024; Civic Economics, 2012). Spend it at a national chain and the same dollar circulates only about 1.2 to 1.4 times before it leaves town. Why the gap? Because the local brewery pays local people their wages, buys ingredients from nearby farms when it can, and keeps its profit in the hands of owners who live and spend right there. The national chain ships much of its profit back to a headquarters far away.
So the local brewery is not just selling you a beer. It is pumping the same dollar through the neighborhood two, three, even four times. The chain mostly sends it out of town.
Step back and you will see this is the same split we keep meeting in this season. On one side, a Trappist abbey that brews only as much beer as it needs to support its community, no advertising, no race to grow. On the other, a public company answering to shareholders, chasing more market share every quarter. One asks, how much do we need? The other asks, how much can we sell? Same beer in the glass, two completely different ideas of what a brewery is for.

[PICTURE] Picture two breweries side by side. The giant is a tower of cans stacked to the ceiling, with a giant billboard out front and a pipe running its profit straight out of town. The craft brewery next door is a small room with a bar, a few local farmers dropping off grain, and a dollar bouncing around the block again and again before it ever leaves.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: a few giants like AB InBev hold roughly 70 percent of U.S. beer volume on advertising and scale, while more than 9,500 craft breweries hold about 25 percent on taprooms and word of mouth, and a local brewery returns roughly 2 to 4 times the local activity of a national chain's 1.2 to 1.4 times (Brewers Association, 2024; Civic Economics, 2012). The smaller the brewery, the more your dollar stays home.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does a local craft brewery do more for its town's economy than a national chain spending the same money? [Beat] Because the local brewery pays local wages, buys local ingredients, and keeps profits local, so each dollar circulates roughly 2 to 4 times instead of the chain's 1.2 to 1.4 times (Brewers Association, 2024; Civic Economics, 2012).

## Key Takeaways
- A handful of giants like AB InBev hold roughly 70 percent of U.S. beer volume, spending billions on advertising and running on volume and thin margins (Brewers Association, 2024).
- More than 9,500 small craft breweries hold about 25 percent of volume, relying on taproom sales and word of mouth (Brewers Association, 2024).
- A local craft brewery returns roughly 2 to 4 times the local economic activity of the same spending, versus about 1.2 to 1.4 times for a national chain (Brewers Association, 2024; Civic Economics, 2012).
- The reason is that local breweries pay local wages, buy local ingredients, and keep profits local, while chains send profit to a distant headquarters.
- It is the same contrast as a Trappist abbey brewing for community sufficiency versus a public company chasing market share.

## Sources
- Brewers Association. (2024). National beer sales and production data. https://www.brewersassociation.org/statistics-and-data/
- Civic Economics. (2012). Indie impact study series: A national comparative survey. https://civiceconomics.com/indie-impact.html

title: The Hymn to Ninkasi
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze the oldest written recipe as a text that fuses practical and spiritual knowledge.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we followed the money in beer. Two quick questions.
One: what is the local multiplier effect? [Beat] It is how many times a dollar spent locally circulates through the community before it leaves, and craft breweries generate more of it than national chains.
Two: roughly how do macro and craft beer split the market? [Beat] Macro holds most of the volume, while craft holds a much smaller slice.

[HOOK] The oldest written recipe we have is not for bread. It is for beer. And here is the strange part. That recipe is also a prayer. The same text that tells you how to brew is praising a goddess at the same time, in the same lines.

[TEACH] The text is called the Hymn to Ninkasi, and it was written around 1800 BCE in Sumerian (Civil, 1964). Ninkasi was the goddess of beer, and this poem is the oldest known written recipe in human history. It does two jobs at once. It is a prayer to the goddess, and it is a complete brewing manual.
Walk through what it actually describes. It tells you how to make the bappir, a special bread baked for brewing. It tells you about the malting, where the grain is soaked and allowed to sprout. It tells you about the mash, the fermentation, and even the serving of the finished beer (Civil, 1964). So this is a real set of instructions. A brewer could follow the steps.
Now the literary point, and this is the heart of the lesson. The Sumerians did not separate the how-to from the holy. They put the recipe and the prayer in one text. To them, knowing how to brew and honoring the goddess of brewing were not two different activities. Practical knowledge and spiritual belief were treated as one thing, braided together in a single poem. When we read it today, we often want to split it apart, the technical part here, the religious part there. The text refuses to be split. That refusal is the message.
There is one more detail worth holding onto. The beer goddess was female. The text praises a woman for the work of brewing (Civil, 1964). That points to something real in history. For most of human history, brewing and food production were largely women's work, done in the home and the temple. A female goddess of beer is not a small accident. It is a window into who actually made the beer.

[PICTURE] Picture a single clay tablet pressed with wedge-shaped marks. Read down one side and it is a hymn, full of praise for Ninkasi. Read the very same lines and it is a checklist, bake the bappir, soak the grain, build the mash, let it ferment, pour it out. One tablet. Two truths at once. The recipe and the prayer are the same words.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Hymn to Ninkasi, written around 1800 BCE, is the oldest known written recipe, and it is a prayer to the goddess of beer and a full brewing manual in one text (Civil, 1964). The Sumerians wrote practical knowledge and spiritual belief as a single act, and the beer goddess was female, which reflects women's long role in brewing.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does it matter that the Hymn to Ninkasi is a recipe and a prayer at the same time? [Beat] Because it shows the Sumerians treated practical knowledge and spiritual belief as one thing, not two separate categories (Civil, 1964).

## Key Takeaways
- The Hymn to Ninkasi, written in Sumerian around 1800 BCE, is the oldest known written recipe (Civil, 1964).
- It is at once a prayer to Ninkasi, the goddess of beer, and a complete brewing manual covering the bappir bread, malting, mash, fermentation, and serving (Civil, 1964).
- Combining a recipe and a prayer shows a culture that treated practical and spiritual knowledge as one act.
- The beer goddess was female, which connects to women's long historical role in brewing and food production (Civil, 1964).

## Sources
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago.

title: Selling Prohibition
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze the persuasive rhetoric that drove the Prohibition movement.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read the Hymn to Ninkasi. Two quick questions.
One: why did the Sumerians combine a recipe and a prayer in one text? [Beat] Because they treated practical knowledge and spiritual belief as one act, not two separate categories (Civil, 1964).
Two: what does a female beer goddess suggest? [Beat] It points to women's long historical role in brewing and food production (Civil, 1964).

[HOOK] How do you talk a whole country into banning a drink it has enjoyed for thousands of years? You do it with words, carefully chosen. The group that pulled this off ran one of the most effective persuasion campaigns in American history, and we can take their methods apart and see how they worked.

[TEACH] The group was the Anti-Saloon League, active from 1893 to 1933, and it was one of the most effective lobbying organizations the country had ever seen (Okrent, 2010). To understand why, look at the three kinds of appeal it used, because it used all three at once.
First, pathos, the appeal to feeling. The League told stories of families destroyed by alcoholism, of children who suffered because of a parent's drinking (Okrent, 2010). These stories hit the heart before the mind could argue back.
Second, ethos, the appeal to credibility. The League aligned itself with Protestant church leaders and with advocates for women's rights (Okrent, 2010). When trusted voices stand behind a message, the message borrows their trust.
Third, logos, the appeal to logic and evidence. The League pointed to statistics on accidents, on crime, and on poverty, all tied to alcohol (Okrent, 2010). Numbers gave the campaign the look of cold, settled fact.
Now the part worth reading most carefully, the framing. The League tied alcohol to domestic violence, to harm done to women and children (Okrent, 2010). Once that link was set, opposing Prohibition no longer looked like a simple policy disagreement. It could be cast as taking the side of the men who hurt their families. That is a sophisticated move. It does not just argue for the ban. It changes what it means to argue against it. If you speak up for the other side, you have to fight off the suggestion that you support harm to women and children. Framing like that does not win the debate. It quietly decides what the debate is about before anyone opens their mouth.
This is why we read it critically. The harms the League named were real, and that matters. But noticing how a message is built, the feeling, the credibility, the numbers, and above all the frame, is the skill that lets you judge any persuasive campaign on its merits instead of being swept along by it.

[PICTURE] Picture a stack of pamphlets on a table. One shows a weeping family, that is pathos. One quotes a respected minister, that is ethos. One is packed with charts of crime and accidents, that is logos. And stamped across all of them is the frame, drinking equals harm to women and children, so that picking up the other side feels like picking the wrong side before you have read a word.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Anti-Saloon League (1893 to 1933) drove Prohibition using pathos, ethos, and logos together, and its sharpest move was framing alcohol as harm to women and children, so that opposing Prohibition could be cast as supporting that harm (Okrent, 2010). Reading the frame is how you read the campaign.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why was tying alcohol to domestic violence such a powerful rhetorical move? [Beat] Because it framed opposing Prohibition as supporting harm to women and children, making the other side hard to take without seeming to endorse that harm (Okrent, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The Anti-Saloon League (1893 to 1933) was one of the most effective lobbying organizations in American history (Okrent, 2010).
- It used pathos (stories of ruined families), ethos (church leaders and womens-rights advocates), and logos (statistics on accidents, crime, and poverty) together (Okrent, 2010).
- Its framing strategy tied alcohol to domestic violence, so opposing Prohibition could be cast as supporting harm to women and children (Okrent, 2010).
- Reading how a message is built, especially its frame, is the skill for judging any persuasive campaign critically.

## Sources
- Okrent, D. (2010). Last call: The rise and fall of Prohibition. Scribner.

title: Reading a Craft Beer Label
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how craft-beer marketing builds identity through storytelling.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we took apart the Prohibition campaign. Two quick questions.
One: name one rhetorical appeal the Anti-Saloon League used. [Beat] Pathos, ethos, or logos, the appeal to feeling, to credibility, or to evidence (Okrent, 2010).
Two: what was the League's framing strategy? [Beat] It tied alcohol to harm against women and children, so opposing Prohibition seemed to support that harm (Okrent, 2010).

[HOOK] Pick up a can of craft beer and read everything on it. The name, the picture, the little paragraph on the back about two friends and a garage. None of that tells you how the beer tastes. So what is it doing there? It is telling you a story, and the story is doing real work.

[TEACH] Modern craft-beer marketing builds identity through narrative (Acitelli, 2013). The beer is the product, but the story is how a brewery stands apart from a thousand others. Look at the moves it uses.
There is the founder story, the two friends in a garage, the dream that became a brewery. Its job is authenticity, to make the brand feel like real people rather than a faceless company (Acitelli, 2013).
There is the local connection, the beer named for the river that runs through town. Its job is community loyalty, to make buying the beer feel like supporting home (Acitelli, 2013).
There is ingredient sourcing, the line about locally grown barley and carefully chosen hops. Its job is to signal quality and values, to say this brewery cares about what goes in the can (Acitelli, 2013).
There is name and logo mythology, the odd name and the striking picture built around a local legend. Its job is simply to stand out on a crowded shelf (Acitelli, 2013).
And there is heritage appeal, the claim of brewing an old family recipe for generations. Its job is to borrow the weight of tradition (Acitelli, 2013).
Now the critical-reading skill, which is the point of the whole lesson. Your task as a reader is to separate what can be verified from what is constructed. Some of the story is checkable. Did two people really found this place? Is the barley really local? Other parts are built to make you feel a certain way, and they may be true, partly true, or just well chosen. A useful exercise is to compare the stories told by local breweries with those told by national brands (Acitelli, 2013). Notice what each one emphasizes, the words it puts up front, and notice what it leaves out, the parts of the story that quietly go missing. What a label does not say is often as revealing as what it does.
None of this means the stories are lies. Many founder stories are genuinely true. It means a label is a text, written on purpose, and like any text it can be read closely instead of just believed.

[PICTURE] Picture two cans side by side. One is from a brewery down the street, named for the local river, with a paragraph about two friends and where the grain was grown. One is from a national brand, all heritage and tradition. Read them like two essays. Underline what you could check. Circle what is built to make you feel. The gap between those two marks is where critical reading lives.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: craft-beer marketing constructs identity through narrative, using the founder story, the local connection, ingredient sourcing, name and logo mythology, and heritage appeal, and the reader's job is to separate what can be verified from what is constructed (Acitelli, 2013). Compare local and national stories, and notice what is emphasized and what is left out.

[CHECK] Quick check. When you read a craft beer label critically, what are you trying to separate? [Beat] What can be verified from what is constructed, the checkable facts from the parts built to make you feel a certain way (Acitelli, 2013).

## Key Takeaways
- Craft-beer marketing constructs identity through narrative, not through taste claims (Acitelli, 2013).
- Common moves include the founder story (authenticity), the local connection (community loyalty), ingredient sourcing (quality and values), name and logo mythology (standing out), and heritage appeal (tradition) (Acitelli, 2013).
- The critical-reading skill is separating what can be verified from what is constructed.
- Comparing local breweries with national brands, and noticing what is emphasized and what is left out, sharpens that skill (Acitelli, 2013).

## Sources
- Acitelli, T. (2013). The audacity of hops: The history of America's craft beer revolution. Chicago Review Press.

title: Key Terms: Beer
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 8 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Beer episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Hordeum vulgare** (HOR-dee-um vul-GAR-ee). Barley, the main grain in beer, a temperate crop that prefers poorer soil (Newton et al., 2011).

**Humulus lupulus** (HYOO-myoo-lus LOO-pyoo-lus). Hops, the flower that gives beer bitterness, aroma, and a natural preservative power (Rybacek, 1991).

**Malt.** Grain that has been germinated and dried, the main source of sugar in beer.

**Water chemistry.** The minerals dissolved into local water from the underlying rock, which shape which beer styles a place can brew (Bamforth, 2009).

**Terroir** (teh-RWAHR). The taste of place, how the local environment shapes flavor.

## Social Studies

**Liquid bread.** A nickname for beer, which gave calories, B vitamins, and carbohydrates, especially useful during fasting (Unger, 2004).

**Reinheitsgebot** (RYN-hytes-geh-BOHT). A 1516 Bavarian food-safety law that defined beer as water, barley, and hops.

**Trappist.** Beer brewed within a monastery under strict rules, funding the community and charity, with no advertising (Hieronymus, 2005).

**Temperance movement.** A reform movement, often led by women, that sought to limit or ban alcohol (Okrent, 2010).

**Prohibition.** The United States ban on making, selling, and transporting alcohol from 1920 to 1933, ended by the only repealed Constitutional amendment (Okrent, 2010).

## Economics

**Three-tier system.** A post-Prohibition rule requiring beer to pass from brewer to distributor to retailer, to prevent tied-house monopolies (Brewers Association, 2024).

**Excise tax.** A tax on domestically produced goods, such as the federal tax per barrel of beer.

**Local multiplier effect.** How money spent locally recirculates in a community. A local brewery returns far more than a national chain (Civic Economics, 2012).

**Craft beer.** Beer made by small, independent breweries, often relying on taproom sales and local sourcing (Brewers Association, 2024).

## English Language Arts

**Ethos, pathos, logos.** The three classic rhetorical appeals: credibility, emotion, and logic (Okrent, 2010).

**Framing.** How a message is positioned to shape how it is understood, as Prohibition was framed as a womens-safety issue (Okrent, 2010).

**Narrative construction.** The deliberate building of a story for persuasive effect, as in craft-beer marketing (Acitelli, 2013).

**Primary source.** An original document from the time studied, like the Hymn to Ninkasi or the Code of Hammurabi (Civil, 1964).

## Sources
- Acitelli, T. (2013). The audacity of hops: The history of America's craft beer revolution. Chicago Review Press.
- Bamforth, C. W. (2009). Beer: Tap into the art and science of brewing (3rd ed.). Oxford University Press.
- Brewers Association. (2024). National beer sales and production data. https://www.brewersassociation.org/statistics-and-data/
- Civic Economics. (2012). Indie impact study series: A national comparative survey. https://civiceconomics.com/indie-impact.html
- Civil, M. (1964). A hymn to the beer goddess and a drinking song. In Studies presented to A. Leo Oppenheim (pp. 67-89). Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago.
- Hieronymus, S. (2005). Brew like a monk. Brewers Publications.
- Newton, A. C., Flavell, A. J., George, T. S., Leat, P., Mullholland, B., Ramsay, L., & Swanston, J. S. (2011). Crops that feed the world 4. Barley: A resilient crop? Food Security, 3(2), 141-178.
- Okrent, D. (2010). Last call: The rise and fall of Prohibition. Scribner.
- Rybacek, V. (Ed.). (1991). Hop production. Elsevier.
- Unger, R. W. (2004). Beer in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance. University of Pennsylvania Press.

title: Cumulative Review: Beer
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Beer episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Beer's two crops, barley and hops, both grow in the temperate band of about 35 to 55 degrees (Newton et al., 2011; Rybacek, 1991). And the single biggest reason a city brews one style and not another is its water, set by the rock beneath it, so soft sandstone water made Plzen's Pilsner, hard limestone water made Dublin's stout, and gypsum water made Burton's IPA (Bamforth, 2009). That whole map is now sliding north as the climate warms (Mika et al., 2023).

Now social studies. Beer is older than writing. People may have farmed grain to brew before they farmed to bake, and the Code of Hammurabi regulated beer rations and tavern prices around 1754 BCE (King, 1910). Medieval monasteries kept brewing alive as safe drink, nutrition, and income, a tradition the Trappist abbeys still hold (Unger, 2004; Hieronymus, 2005). And the United States tried to ban alcohol entirely, but Prohibition (1920 to 1933) failed and was repealed by the only Constitutional amendment ever undone (Okrent, 2010).

Then economics. The farmer gets only about 3 to 5 percent of a pint, while brewing, distribution, and retail take the rest, and most states still route beer through a three-tier system born after Prohibition (Brewers Association, 2024). A handful of macro giants hold about 70 percent of U.S. volume, but more than 9,500 craft breweries return far more to their towns through the local multiplier effect (Brewers Association, 2024; Civic Economics, 2012).

Last, language. The oldest written recipe is the Hymn to Ninkasi, a prayer and a brewing manual in one (Civil, 1964). The Anti-Saloon League sold Prohibition with ethos, pathos, and logos, framing alcohol as a threat to women and children (Okrent, 2010). And modern craft labels build identity through founder stories you can learn to read critically (Acitelli, 2013).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Beer is not a modern indulgence. It is older than cities, shaped by the rock under the ground, woven into law and faith and policy, and carried in language from a Sumerian hymn to a craft-beer can. Liquid bread holds the whole world, and, as the season reminds us honestly, it carries real harm too.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Why is beer called liquid bread, and what makes it more than a drink? [Beat] It gave real nutrition, and it is woven into the history of cities, law, faith, economics, and language, so it carries the whole world inside it (Unger, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: barley and hops are temperate crops, and local water chemistry, set by geology, decides beer style (Bamforth, 2009).
- Social studies: beer is older than writing, central to early law, kept alive by monasteries, and the subject of the failed Prohibition experiment (King, 1910; Okrent, 2010).
- Economics: farmers get the least, the three-tier system shapes the market, and local craft breweries return more to their towns (Brewers Association, 2024).
- Language: from the Hymn to Ninkasi to Prohibition rhetoric to craft labels, beer is wrapped in persuasive and sacred texts (Civil, 1964; Acitelli, 2013).

## Sources
- Acitelli, T. (2013). The audacity of hops: The history of America's craft beer revolution. Chicago Review Press.
- Bamforth, C. W. (2009). Beer: Tap into the art and science of brewing (3rd ed.). Oxford University Press.

title: Sources and Further Reading: Beer
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The episode is educational and includes honest sources on alcohol's health harms.
