title: Knowledge Check: Beer
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 8: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, how geology shapes a glass and whether Prohibition was a failure, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"In what latitude band do barley and hops grow best?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"About 35 to 55 degrees, north or south"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Only at the equator"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Above 70 degrees North"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Anywhere on Earth"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Both barley and hops are temperate crops that grow best between about 35 and 55 degrees, which is why the great brewing traditions grew up in the cooler middle latitudes.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why does Indiana, which sits within the barley latitudes, grow little barley?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Its soil is too rich and fertile, and barley prefers poorer soil"},{"id":"q2b","text":"It is too cold"},{"id":"q2c","text":"It has no water"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Barley is illegal there"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Barley does best with a challenge, not rich soil, so fertile Indiana grows mostly corn and soybeans instead, even though it lies within the barley latitudes.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What do hops contribute to beer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Bitterness, aroma, and a natural preservative power"},{"id":"q3b","text":"All of the alcohol"},{"id":"q3c","text":"The water"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Nothing; they are decorative"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Hops (Humulus lupulus) give beer its bitterness, aroma, and a natural preservative effect, and they need long summer days and a winter dormancy.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What mostly decides which beer style a city can brew?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"The minerals in the local water, set by the underlying rock"},{"id":"q4b","text":"The color of the brewery"},{"id":"q4c","text":"The price of beer"},{"id":"q4d","text":"The size of the city"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Beer is mostly water, and the minerals dissolved from the local rock shape what styles are possible. Brewers worked with the water they had, so style follows geology.","citation":"Lesson: How Geology Makes the Beer"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Why did Plzen become the home of pale, crisp Pilsner?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Its soft water over sandstone is very low in minerals"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Its hard water is full of gypsum"},{"id":"q5c","text":"It has no water"},{"id":"q5d","text":"A king ordered it"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Plzen sits on sandstone, giving very soft water low in everything, which makes a pale, crisp, clear beer, the Pilsner that took the city's name.","citation":"Lesson: How Geology Makes the Beer"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Why did Burton-upon-Trent become famous for hoppy IPA?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Its very hard gypsum water, high in sulfate, sharpens hop bitterness"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Its soft water makes pale lager"},{"id":"q6c","text":"It is closest to the hop fields"},{"id":"q6d","text":"It banned all other styles"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Burton's water comes from gypsum and is high in calcium sulfate, and sulfate sharpens hop bitterness, which is why the city became the home of the bitter, hoppy India Pale Ale.","citation":"Lesson: How Geology Makes the Beer"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"How is climate change moving beer geography?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Barley regions are sliding north by about 4 to 8 kilometers per decade, and new regions open in the far north"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Beer can now grow at the equator only"},{"id":"q7c","text":"Nothing is changing"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Barley regions are moving toward the equator"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Barley regions are creeping north by an estimated 4 to 8 kilometers per decade, hop quality is falling in parts of Europe, and new areas in Scotland, Scandinavia, and Canada are becoming viable.","citation":"Lesson: Beer Geography Is Shifting"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What is the Hymn to Ninkasi?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"The oldest known written recipe, a Sumerian prayer to the beer goddess that is also a brewing manual"},{"id":"q8b","text":"A modern beer brand"},{"id":"q8c","text":"A Prohibition-era law"},{"id":"q8d","text":"A type of hop"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Written around 1800 BCE, the Hymn to Ninkasi is the oldest known written recipe, a prayer to the goddess of beer that doubles as a complete brewing manual.","citation":"Lesson: The Hymn to Ninkasi"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What does the Code of Hammurabi show about beer in ancient Mesopotamia?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Beer was important enough to regulate, with daily rations by rank and price controls on taverns"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Beer was banned entirely"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Beer had no role in society"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Only kings could drink beer"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"The Code of Hammurabi (about 1754 BCE) set beer rations that rose with rank, controlled tavern prices, and punished watering down beer, showing how central beer was to daily and economic life.","citation":"Lesson: Liquid Bread and the First Cities"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"Why did medieval monasteries become centers of brewing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Fermented beer was safer than city water, it gave nutrition during fasting, and it funded charity"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Monks were forbidden to do any other work"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Beer was the only legal drink"},{"id":"q10d","text":"They disliked wine"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Monasteries brewed because fermented beer was safer than often-contaminated city water, beer as liquid bread gave calories and B vitamins during fasting, and sales funded charity and education, with the motto ora et labora making work spiritual.","citation":"Lesson: The Monks Who Kept Brewing"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What does the Trappist brewing model require?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Brewing within the monastery, monastic involvement, funding the community and charity, and no advertising"},{"id":"q11b","text":"Maximum production and global advertising"},{"id":"q11c","text":"Selling to the highest bidder"},{"id":"q11d","text":"Brewing only lager"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"A Trappist beer must be brewed within the abbey with monastic involvement, with revenue supporting the community and charity, and without commercial advertising, the opposite of a giant like AB InBev.","citation":"Lesson: The Monks Who Kept Brewing"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What was the Reinheitsgebot of 1516?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"An early Bavarian food-safety law defining beer as water, barley, and hops"},{"id":"q12b","text":"A modern marketing slogan"},{"id":"q12c","text":"A tax on hops"},{"id":"q12d","text":"A ban on all beer"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The Reinheitsgebot, enacted in Bavaria in 1516, was one of the first food-safety laws, defining beer as water, barley, and hops.","citation":"Lesson: The Monks Who Kept Brewing"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What does the repeal of Prohibition make the Twenty-First Amendment?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"The only Constitutional amendment ever repealed"},{"id":"q13b","text":"The first amendment ever passed"},{"id":"q13c","text":"A state law, not an amendment"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Still in force as Prohibition"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"The Eighteenth Amendment created Prohibition in 1920; the Twenty-First repealed it in 1933, making it the only Constitutional amendment ever repealed.","citation":"Lesson: Prohibition, the Experiment That Failed"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What were the results of Prohibition by the numbers?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"Licensed breweries fell from about 1,300 to near zero, alcohol tax revenue vanished, and organized crime grew explosively"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Crime fell to zero and tax revenue rose"},{"id":"q14c","text":"Nothing measurable changed"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Breweries doubled"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Under Prohibition, legal breweries dropped from about 1,300 to near zero, federal alcohol tax revenue went to zero, and organized crime grew explosively before declining after repeal.","citation":"Lesson: Prohibition, the Experiment That Failed"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"In the beer supply chain, about what share of a pint's price reaches the barley farmer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"About 3 to 5 percent"},{"id":"q15b","text":"About 50 percent"},{"id":"q15c","text":"About 90 percent"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Zero"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Barley farmers capture only about 3 to 5 percent of a pint's price, while brewing takes about 25 to 35 percent and retail or the taproom about 30 to 40 percent.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid for a Pint"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"Why did most states adopt the three-tier system after Prohibition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"To prevent breweries from owning retail and recreating the exploitative tied-house saloons"},{"id":"q16b","text":"To make beer cheaper"},{"id":"q16c","text":"To ban craft beer"},{"id":"q16d","text":"To eliminate all taxes"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"The three-tier system separates brewer, distributor, and retailer to stop breweries from controlling retail, as they did in the pre-Prohibition tied-house saloons, though it adds cost for small brewers.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid for a Pint"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"How does the local multiplier effect compare a local craft brewery with a national chain?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"A local brewery returns about 2 to 4 times the local activity, versus about 1.2 to 1.4 for a national chain"},{"id":"q17b","text":"They are identical"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Chains return more locally"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Neither has any local effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"A local craft brewery recirculates roughly 2 to 4 times the local economic activity of the same spending, against about 1.2 to 1.4 times for a national chain, because it pays local wages, buys local ingredients, and keeps profits local.","citation":"Lesson: Macro Beer and Craft Beer"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"How do macro and craft brewing compare in the U.S. market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"A handful of macro giants hold about 70 percent of volume; more than 9,500 craft breweries hold about 25 percent"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Craft holds 90 percent of volume"},{"id":"q18c","text":"There are only macro breweries"},{"id":"q18d","text":"They split the market evenly"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"A few macro companies like AB InBev hold roughly 70 percent of U.S. beer volume, while more than 9,500 small craft breweries hold about 25 percent, relying on taprooms and word of mouth.","citation":"Lesson: Macro Beer and Craft Beer"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"Why did the Sumerians combine a recipe and a prayer in the Hymn to Ninkasi?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"They treated practical knowledge and spiritual belief as one"},{"id":"q19b","text":"They could not write two separate documents"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It was a joke"},{"id":"q19d","text":"There was no recipe in it"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"By making one text both a brewing manual and a prayer to the beer goddess, the Sumerians showed that practical knowledge and spiritual belief were woven together for them.","citation":"Lesson: The Hymn to Ninkasi"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"Which three rhetorical appeals did the Anti-Saloon League use to drive Prohibition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Pathos (stories of harmed families), ethos (church and reform allies), and logos (statistics on harm)"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Only humor"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Only price cuts"},{"id":"q20d","text":"No persuasion at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"The Anti-Saloon League combined pathos (stories of families destroyed by alcohol), ethos (alignment with church leaders and reformers), and logos (statistics on accidents, crime, and poverty).","citation":"Lesson: Selling Prohibition"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What was the Anti-Saloon League's key framing strategy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Tying alcohol to domestic violence, so opposing Prohibition looked like supporting harm to women and children"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Promising cheaper beer"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Praising saloons"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Ignoring families entirely"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"By framing alcohol as a cause of domestic violence, the League made opposing Prohibition hard to defend, a sophisticated rhetorical move worth reading critically.","citation":"Lesson: Selling Prohibition"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What is the key skill when reading a craft-beer marketing label?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Separating what can be verified from what is a constructed story"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Believing every claim"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Ignoring the label"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Only checking the price"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Craft marketing builds identity through founder stories, local connections, and heritage appeals. The critical skill is telling verified facts from constructed narrative.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Craft Beer Label"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What is malt?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Grain that has been germinated and dried, the main source of sugar in beer"},{"id":"q23b","text":"A type of hop"},{"id":"q23c","text":"The yeast"},{"id":"q23d","text":"A tax"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Malt is grain, usually barley, that has been germinated and dried, providing the sugars that yeast ferments into alcohol.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is fermentation, the core process in beer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"The conversion of sugar to alcohol by yeast"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Boiling water"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Freezing the grain"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Adding color"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Fermentation is the process in which yeast converts sugar into alcohol, the chemical heart of every fermented beverage in this season.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"Where was the earliest known evidence of a fermented beverage found?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Jiahu, China, around 7000 BCE"},{"id":"q25b","text":"London, around 1700 CE"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Rome, around 100 CE"},{"id":"q25d","text":"There is no such evidence"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"The earliest known evidence of a fermented beverage comes from Jiahu, China, around 7000 BCE, older than many civilizations.","citation":"Lesson: Liquid Bread and the First Cities"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Why is beer nicknamed liquid bread?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"It provided calories, B vitamins, and carbohydrates, especially useful during fasting"},{"id":"q26b","text":"It is baked like bread"},{"id":"q26c","text":"It contains no alcohol"},{"id":"q26d","text":"It is solid"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Beer earned the name liquid bread because it provided real nutrition, calories, B vitamins, and carbohydrates, which made it valuable to monks during fasting.","citation":"Lesson: The Monks Who Kept Brewing"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What real problems did the temperance movement respond to?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Alcohol abuse linked to domestic violence, poverty, and workplace accidents"},{"id":"q27b","text":"A shortage of beer"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Too many breweries closing"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Nothing real"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"The temperance movement, often led by women, responded to real harms: alcohol abuse tied to domestic violence, poverty, and workplace accidents. Prohibition's failure does not erase those problems.","citation":"Lesson: Prohibition, the Experiment That Failed"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Why is a craft taproom often more profitable than selling through distribution?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"The taproom captures both the brewing margin and the retail margin"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Taprooms pay no rent"},{"id":"q28c","text":"Distribution is free"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Taprooms charge less"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"By selling directly to customers, a craft taproom keeps both the brewing margin (about 25 to 35 percent) and the retail margin (about 30 to 40 percent), instead of giving the retail share away.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid for a Pint"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What does it tell us that the Sumerian goddess of beer was female?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"It connects to women's historical role in brewing and food production"},{"id":"q29b","text":"That only men brewed"},{"id":"q29c","text":"That beer was forbidden to women"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"A female beer goddess reflects women's long historical role in brewing and food production, work that was often central to the household and the temple.","citation":"Lesson: The Hymn to Ninkasi"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"How does water become mineral-rich or soft in a brewing town?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Rain dissolves minerals from the underlying rock as it soaks through the ground"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Brewers add minerals by law"},{"id":"q30c","text":"Water is born mineral-rich"},{"id":"q30d","text":"The climate sets it directly"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"As rain soaks through the ground, it dissolves minerals from the rock, so sandstone gives soft water and limestone or gypsum gives hard water, shaping the beer.","citation":"Lesson: How Geology Makes the Beer"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Which contrast shows two opposite brewing business models?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Tiny Trappist Westvleteren, brewing for community sufficiency, versus giant AB InBev, chasing market share"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Two identical global brands"},{"id":"q31c","text":"A brewery versus a bakery"},{"id":"q31d","text":"There is no contrast"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"A Trappist abbey like Westvleteren brews a tiny amount to fund its community and charity, while AB InBev produces vast volumes for shareholder returns, two opposite purposes for the same product.","citation":"Lesson: Macro Beer and Craft Beer"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"Some archaeologists argue that beer may have done what for early humans?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Helped motivate the move to farming and settling down"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Caused the dinosaurs to vanish"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Prevented all agriculture"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Nothing of importance"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Some archaeologists argue the desire for beer helped pull people into cultivating grain and settling down, meaning people may have farmed to brew before they farmed to bake.","citation":"Lesson: Liquid Bread and the First Cities"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What is terroir, a term beer shares with wine?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"The taste of place, how the local environment shapes flavor"},{"id":"q33b","text":"A type of yeast"},{"id":"q33c","text":"A brewing tax"},{"id":"q33d","text":"A French brewery"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Terroir means the taste of place, the way local geography, climate, and water shape a flavor. In beer it shows up most clearly in the water chemistry of each brewing town.","citation":"Lesson: How Geology Makes the Beer"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"Which narrative element is common in craft-beer marketing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"A founder story that creates authenticity, like 'two friends brewing in a garage'"},{"id":"q34b","text":"A list of competitors"},{"id":"q34c","text":"The chemical formula for ethanol"},{"id":"q34d","text":"No story at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Craft marketing builds identity with elements like the founder story, the local connection, and heritage appeals. The founder story creates a sense of authenticity and relatability.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Craft Beer Label"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Why are barley and hops described as crops of the temperate world?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Both grow best in the cooler middle latitudes, not the tropics"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Both grow only at the equator"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Both grow underwater"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Both grow in deserts"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Barley and hops both belong to the temperate band of about 35 to 55 degrees, which is why the great beer cultures grew up in northern Europe and the northern United States.","citation":"Lesson: The Grain Belt and the Hop Belt"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What is the federal excise tax structure on beer for small versus large brewers?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Small brewers pay a much lower rate per barrel on their first barrels than large producers do"},{"id":"q36b","text":"Small brewers pay more than large producers"},{"id":"q36c","text":"There is no beer tax"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Only importers pay tax"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Federal excise tax favors small brewers, who pay about 3.50 dollars per barrel on their first 60,000 barrels, while large producers pay about 18 dollars per barrel, with a barrel being 31 gallons.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid for a Pint"}]}
